title: Mathematical Foundations for Economics Students
text:
Preface
This website is a development in progress to serve as an open-access mathematical compendium covering mathematical topics fundamental for any student of economics.
Version 0.0 of this resource is to include Trigonometry, Calculus I, Calculus II, and applications of such topics in the context of Economics.
This resource is designed for long-term expansion across additional, related subjects that will build upon the necessary, basic principles that Version 0.0 introduces.
Since this is an independent project that is following concurrently with my own studies, I’d like to ask that you please pardon the ongoing construction. All errors are my own.

title: 1  Basics of Trigonometry
text:
1.1 The Three Primary Functions
In mathematics, there are six fundamental trigonometric functions. For simplicity, we will begin with the three primary functions to establish the basic framework and then introduce their reciprocals later on. The three primary functions are as follows with their classic mnemonic for easy recall. \[
\begin{array}{ccc}
\textbf{SOH} & \textbf{CAH} & \textbf{TOA} \\[6pt]
\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} &
\cos\theta = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} &
\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}
\end{array}
\]
You can think of sine, cosine, and tangent as ratios that answer a very specific question: given an angle \(\theta\), what are the proportions of the sides of any right triangle that contains that angle? It doesn’t matter how large or small the triangle is. For example, the ratio of opposite to hypotenuse is always the same number: \(\sin\theta\). These functions don’t give us side lengths, but rather, a ratio that is fixed to that angle. Essentially, sine, cosine, and tangent are a way to encode the shape of a triangle using a single number, the angle. The pythagorean theorem, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), finds missing sides of a right triangle, with \(a\) being the side opposite from the angle, \(b\) being the side next to the angle, and \(c\) being the hypotenuse, or the longest side. Because of this connection, we can similarly write the three primary functions as: \[
\sin\theta = \frac{a}{c} \qquad
\cos\theta = \frac{b}{c} \qquad
\tan\theta = \frac{a}{b}
\]

title: 1  Basics of Trigonometry
1  Basics of Trigonometry
text:
1.3 Special Right Triangles
Special right triangles are specific triangles with fixed angles, making their side lengths intuitive without even having to use the pythagorean theorem. There are two special right triangles: the 30-60-90 and the 45-45-90. These are special because they are the only angles where the ratios come out as clean, exact numbers. Its helpful to think of them as geometric proofs of the ratios and not just a memory device. Because of their simplicity, they make it easier to analyze the unit circle and evaluate trigonometric functions.

1.3.1 Special Right Triangle #1: 30-60-90
The first special right triangle has a base length of 1, a height of \(\sqrt{3}\), and a hypotenuse of 2. Its smallest angle measures \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) degrees and the remaining angle measures \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) degrees. Below are the evaluations of sine, cosine, and tangent for the given angles:


\[
\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{1}{2} \qquad
\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{\sqrt3}{2}
\] \[
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \qquad
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} = \frac{1}{2}
\] \[
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt3} \qquad
\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} = \frac{\sqrt3}{1}
\]
Think of these evaluations as a way of “looking up” the ratio that corresponds to the angle. It doesn’t matter if the hypotenuse is 1 centimeter or 1 kilometer, in any right triangle where one angle is \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), for example, the fraction of the hypotenuse that is the adjacent side will always be exactly half. There is no arithmetic being done or formula we are plugging values into. These evaluations read a geometric fact because these ratios will hold for the specified angle no matter what.

title: 1  Basics of Trigonometry
text:
1.4 The Unit Circle
The unit circle is a circle of radius \(r = 1\) centered at the origin of the Cartesian plane. It is the foundation of trigonometry and, by extension, of much of calculus.
For any angle \(\theta\) measured counterclockwise from the positive \(x\)-axis, the corresponding point on the unit circle is:
\[
(\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta)
\]

title: 3  Inverse Trigonometry
text:
3.1 Inverse Trigonometric Functions
In the previous sections, we used trigonometric functions to answer the question: given an angle \(\theta\), what is the corresponding ratio? The inverse trigonometric functions reverse this question entirely: given a ratio, what is the angle that produced it?. The functions are the following:
\[
\sin^{-1}(x)=\arcsin(x)
\]
\[
\cos^{-1}(x)=\arccos(x)
\] \[
\tan^{-1}(x)=\arctan(x)
\]
Think of arcsine, arccosine, and arctangent as “undoing” sine, cosine, and tangent. For example, we know that \(\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\). The inverse sine function lets us ask this in reverse: which angle \(\theta\) satisfies \(\sin(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\)? The answer, written \(\arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}\), is exactly that angle.
However, a subtlety arises immediately. For a function to be invertible, it must return exactly one value, but since sine, cosine, and tangent are periodic functions, each ratio corresponds to infinitely many angles. For example, \(\sin(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\) is satisfied not just by \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) but by \(\frac{5\pi}{6}\), and by infinitely many others. For these inverses to be actual functions, we must impose restrictions on sine, cosine, and tangent so that any unique input to these functions has a unique output.

title: 3  Inverse Trigonometry
text:
3.2 Principal Values
These restrictions manifest in something called the principal value branch, or the principal interval. The intervals define the single agreed-upon output angle chosen from the infinitely many that would technically satisfy the equation. These outputs are called principal values. Just as \(\sqrt{4}\) returns \(+2\) by convention rather than \(-2\), \(\arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\) returns \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) rather than \(\frac{5\pi}{6}\) because \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) falls within the principal interval \(\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\). Without a principal interval for each primary trig function, it would be impossible to have inverses of these functions as we would get infinitely many answers for any given degree.

title: 4  Introduction to Derivatives
text:
4.1 Derivatives
A derivative measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function. At any given point, the derivative is the slope of the line tangent to the curve at that point. This is different from the average rate of change between two points, which is the slope of the secant line connecting them. The following graph depicts both concepts:

title: 4  Introduction to Derivatives
text:
4.2 Differentiation Rules


Constant & Power \[\frac{d}{dx}(a)=0\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(x^n)=nx^{n-1}\]
Exponential & Logarithmic \[\frac{d}{dx}(e^x)=e^x\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(a^x)=a^x \ln(a)\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\ln x)=\frac{1}{x}\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\log_a x)=\frac{1}{x\ln(a)}\]
Inverse Trigonometric \[\frac{d}{dx}(\arcsin x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\arccos x)=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\arctan x)=\frac{1}{1+x^2}\]



Trigonometric \[\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x)=\cos x\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\cos x)=-\sin x\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\tan x)=\sec^2 x\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\sec x)=\sec x\tan x\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\csc x)=-\csc x\cot x\] \[\frac{d}{dx}(\cot x)=-\csc^2 x\]
Domain Restrictions Recall the three general rules that restrict a domain:

Denominators: \(d \neq 0\)
Even roots: expression inside \(\geq 0\)
Logarithms: expression inside \(> 0\)

Several derivative formulas carry implicit domain restrictions or points where the formula breaks down and the function is not differentiable. This does not mean the function itself is necessarily discontinuous at those points, but rather that the derivative fails to exist there. Recognizing which rules carry these restrictions is an important part of analyzing the full behavior of a function.

title: 5  Integration Techniques
text:
A Note on the Nature of Integration
Integration is often introduced by relating it to differentiation, and while it is technically the inverse of differentiation, the actual process of integration is quite a distinct experience. Differentiation has clean, mechanical rules that reliably produce an answer for any function you encounter. The process of taking a derivative is quite linear: choose from a (short) list of formulaic rules, apply the rules, and simplify your answer with a bit of algebra. Integration, on the other hand, demands a certain level of critical thinking and pattern recognition, and you will soon see that not every integral has a closed-form solution at all. This is all to say that you will save yourself some shock if you avoid building your expectations for integration around the notion that it will be the opposite of taking a derivative.
The process of integration can look and feel a lot like failure. Improving your skills will require you to let go of a desire for perfection and just choose a strategy to dive into the problem-solving process, even if it may be the incorrect one. It is normal to try something, get stuck, backtrack, and try again many times until you arrive at the answer. Learning to integrate is largely learning to tolerate uncertainty and to be resilient amidst the presence of failure.
It is common for there to be a learning curve as we transition from differentiation to integration, but don’t let this discourage you; everytime you solve one of these puzzles, you expand the limits of your mind, add new tools to your toolkit, and get a step closer to mathematical maturity.
The strategies outlined in this chapter cover the most common forms you will encounter, but knowing which strategy to apply (or whether to combine them) is a skill that develops through practice and through pushing through failed attempts.

title: 5  Integration Techniques
text:
5.1 Integration by Substitution
Often referred to as “u-substitution” or “u-sub”, integration by substitution is a technique that essentially undoes the chain rule to evaluate complex integrals. Remember that chain rule is our technique for differentiating composite functions, i.e., a function inside of a function. Similarly, we can use u-sub to find antiderivatives of composite functions.
Take the following integrand as an example: \(\int 2x(x^2+3)^7 \,dx\) Recognize that it takes the classic form of a composite function: \(((f \circ g)(x))\), where \((x^2+3)\) is our \(g(x)\), or our “inner” function in this case. In a similar way to the chain rule, we will choose this part of the function to be our \(u\).

5.1.1 Choosing \(u\): General Heuristics
The most important step in u-substitution is recognizing which part of the integrand to set as \(u\). The following strategies cover the most common forms:

Composite functions — choose the inner function (Strategy 1)
Exponential terms — choose the exponent (Strategy 2)

Fractions — choose the denominator when its derivative appears in the numerator (Strategy 3)
Logarithmic terms — choose the argument of the log (Strategy 4)



5.1.2 Strategy 1: Basic U-Substitution
Example: Evaluate \(\int 2x(x^2+3)^7 \,dx\)
Recognize that \((x^2+3)\) is our inner function \(g(x)\), so we set \(u = x^2 + 3\).
Step 1: Set \(u\) equal to the chosen expression \[u = x^2+3\]
Step 2: Differentiate with respect to \(x\) and isolate \(dx\) \[\frac{du}{dx} = 2x \implies dx = \frac{du}{2x}\]
Step 3: Substitute \(u\) and \(dx\) into the integral \[\int 2x(u)^7 \frac{du}{2x}\]
Notice the \(2x\) terms cancel, leaving: \[\int u^7 \,du\]
Step 4: Integrate with respect to \(u\) \[= \frac{u^8}{8} + C\]
Step 5: Substitute the original expression back in for \(u\) \[= \frac{1}{8}(x^2+3)^8 + C\]
Therefore: \[\int 2x(x^2+3)^7 \,dx = \frac{1}{8}(x^2+3)^8 + C\]


5.1.3 Strategy 2: Single Exponential Form
If there is a single exponential term, we often let the exponent be the \(u\).
Example Evaluate \(\int x^4e^{{7x}^{5}}\,dx\)
Here, recognize that our exponential term is raised to the power \({7x}^{5}\). Let’s choose this as our \(u\) and proceed with the following u-sub steps as we did in the previous example.
Step 1: Set \(u\) equal to the chosen expression \[u = {7x}^{5}\]
Step 2: Differentiate with respect to \(x\) and isolate \(dx\) \[\frac{du}{dx} = 35x^4 \implies dx = \frac{du}{35x^4}\]
Step 3: Substitute \(u\) and \(dx\) into the integral \[\int x^4e^u\frac{du}{35x^4}\]
Notice the \(x^4\) terms cancel, leaving: \[\int e^u\frac{du}{35}\,du\]
Step 4: Integrate with respect to \(u\) \[= \frac{1}{35}e^u + C\]
Step 5: Substitute the original expression back in for \(u\) \[= \frac{1}{35}e^{{7x}^{5}} + C\]
Therefore: \[\int x^4e^{{7x}^{5}}\,dx = \frac{1}{35}e^{{7x}^{5}} + C\]


5.1.4 Strategy 3: Non-Reducible Fractions
If we see a fraction we can’t reduce, then we often let the entire denominator be \(u\).
Example 1 Evaluate \(\int \frac{3x^2+7}{x^3+7x}\,dx\)
There’s no factoring we can do to get this integrand into a simpler form so let’s choose the denominator \(x^3+7x\) as our \(u\) and proceed with our u-sub steps.
Step 1: Set \(u\) equal to the chosen expression \[u = x^3+7x\]
Step 2: Differentiate with respect to \(x\) and isolate \(dx\) \[\frac{du}{dx} = 3x^2+7 \implies dx = \frac{du}{3x^2+7}\]
Step 3: Substitute \(u\) and \(dx\) into the integral \[\int \frac{3x^2+7}{u} \cdot \frac{du}{3x^2+7}\]
Notice the \(x^4\) terms cancel, leaving: \[\int \frac{1}{u}\,du\]
Step 4: Integrate with respect to \(u\) \[\int u^{-1}\,du \implies ln|u| + C\]
Step 5: Substitute the original expression back in for \(u\) \[= ln|x^3+7x| + C\]
Therefore: \[\int \frac{3x^2+7}{x^3+7x}\,dx = ln|x^3+7x| + C\]
Example 2 Evaluate \(\int \tan x\,dx\)
This integrand may not appear as a fraction on the surface, but let’s recall one of our trig identities and recognize that \(\tan x\ = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\), rewriting the integral as: \[\int \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\,dx\] Because of this preliminary rewrite, we can now apply the same strategy, choosing the denominator $x$ as our \(u\) and proceeding with u-sub steps.
Step 1: Set \(u\) equal to the chosen expression \[u = \cos x\]
Step 2: Differentiate with respect to \(x\) and isolate \(dx\) \[\frac{du}{dx} = -\sin x \implies dx = \frac{du}{-\sin x}\]
Step 3: Substitute \(u\) and \(dx\) into the integral \[\int \frac{\sin x}{u} \cdot \frac{du}{-\sin x}\]
Notice the \(\sin x\) terms cancel, leaving: \[\int -\frac{1}{u}\,du\]
Step 4: Integrate with respect to \(u\) \[= -ln|u| + C\]
Step 5: Substitute the original expression back in for \(u\) \[= -ln|\cos x| + C\]
Therefore: \[\int \tan x\,dx = \int \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\,dx = -ln|\cos x| + C\]
<!–> A note as we begin working with integrals: This is only the beginning of some of the “outside-of-the-box” thinking skills Calculus II demands. It is common for there to be a learning curve as we transition from differentiation to integration, but don’t let this discourage you; everytime we solve these puzzles, we expand the limits of our mind, add new tools to our toolkit, and get a step closer to mathematical maturity.–>


5.1.5 Strategy 4: Logarithmic Terms
If there is a log term as part of a multiplication or division, we often let the entire term with the log be \(u\).
Example 1 Evaluate \(\int \frac{\ln(x)+3}{x}\,dx\)
It may be tempting to choose \(x\) as our \(u\) — after all, we just covered this as our strategy for integrating non-reducible fractions. However, the log term in the numerator complicates things. A natural question arises: how do we choose \(u\) when the integral doesn’t obviously fit a pattern we recognize? A useful approach when you get stuck is to choose the “unknown” term—that is, the term for which we don’t have a clean antiderivative formula—as our \(u\). Remembering our antiderivative rules, we can recognize \(\ln(x)\) as the unknown piece of this integrand. Since the constant \(3\) is simply added to \(\ln(x)\) and the two are inseparable in the numerator, it is natural to treat the entire expression \(\ln(x) + 3\) as our \(u\). As we will see, this choice keeps our substitution clean and allows all original variables to cancel neatly.
Step 1: Set \(u\) equal to the chosen expression \[u = \ln(x)+3\]
Step 2: Differentiate with respect to \(x\) and isolate \(dx\) \[\frac{du}{dx} = \frac{1}{x} \implies dx = du \cdot x\]
Step 3: Substitute \(u\) and \(dx\) into the integral \[\int \frac{u}{x}\, du \cdot x\]
Notice the \(x\) terms cancel, leaving: \[\int u \cdot du\]
Step 4: Integrate with respect to \(u\) \[ \frac{u^2}{2} + C\]
Step 5: Substitute the original expression back in for \(u\) \[= \frac{(\ln(x)+3)^2}{2} + C \implies =\frac{1}{2}(\ln(x)+3)^2+C\]
Therefore: \[\int \frac{\ln(x)+3}{x}\,dx = \frac{1}{2}(\ln(x)+3)^2+C\]

title: 6  Optimization
text:
6.1 Key Functions in Economic Optimization
Optimization is the process of finding the maximum or minimum value of a function in the context of an applied problem. In economics, this translates directly to questions every firm and consumer faces: How much should a firm produce to maximize profit? At what price is revenue maximized? How does a consumer allocate a fixed budget to maximize utility? The calculus tools developed in the previous chapter give us a precise, systematic way to answer these questions.
